serving.description: 1 lamb rack portion (approximately 300 g with bone)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of black pepper lamb rack is approximately 300 g including bone, yielding roughly 180–200 g of edible meat.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, domestic, rib, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/4 inch fat, choice, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb rib (roasted, lean and fat, choice grade) used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values adjusted upward for sodium to account for black pepper marinade and soy-based sauce typical of Chinese-style preparation, consistent with standard culinary adaptation methodology. Black pepper and soy sauce contribute additional sodium estimated at +150 mg/100 g over plain roasted lamb. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are anchored to the USDA cooked lamb rib entry. Carbohydrate contribution from black pepper sauce marinade (soy sauce, oyster sauce, sugar) estimated at approximately 3 g/100 g of finished dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for black pepper lamb rack. Lamb and other cooked meats are protein- and fat-dominant foods with negligible glycemic carbohydrates. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care) consistently classify cooked meats as GI ≤ 10 due to absence of significant starch or sugar. A GI of 10 is assigned as a conservative analogous estimate for this meat-dominant dish. The small carbohydrate contribution (~3 g/100 g) from the black pepper sauce (soy sauce, oyster sauce, sugar) is predominantly simple sugars with minimal starch, yielding a glycemic load per 300 g serving of approximately 0.5 g, which is clinically negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce marinade (~480 mg sodium per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g per serving and request reduced-sauce preparation, as the soy-based black pepper marinade contributes approximately 480 mg sodium per 100 g, which can significantly contribute to daily sodium intake targets of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~10) and low carbohydrate content (~3 g/100 g); high protein supports satiety
type2_diabetes.guidance: Black pepper lamb rack has a low glycemic index and minimal carbohydrate content, making it a suitable protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the soy-based sauce contains small amounts of sugar and should be accounted for in total carbohydrate budgeting, particularly for those on strict carbohydrate-controlled meal plans.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~7.2 g/100 g) and cholesterol (~88 mg/100 g) from lamb rib cut
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume black pepper lamb rack in moderation due to its saturated fat content of approximately 7.2 g per 100 g; choosing leaner lamb cuts and limiting portion size to 150 g can help manage saturated fat intake within the recommended limit of less than 10% of total daily energy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Pepper Lamb Rack (黑椒羊扒) is a Chinese-style grilled or pan-seared lamb rack dish marinated and coated in a bold black pepper sauce. The dish features a rack of lamb — typically 2 to 4 ribs per portion — that is marinated in a mixture of crushed black pepper, soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, sesame oil, and sugar before being grilled or seared to achieve a caramelised, peppery crust while retaining a juicy, tender interior. The black pepper sauce imparts a sharp, aromatic heat that complements the rich, slightly gamey flavour of lamb. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served at Chinese seafood restaurants and zi char (煮炒) stalls as a premium meat option, often accompanied by stir-fried vegetables and steamed rice. Nutritionally, black pepper lamb rack is a high-protein, moderate-to-high fat dish. Per 100 g of edible portion, it provides approximately 258 kcal, 24.5 g protein, 16.8 g fat (of which 7.2 g is saturated), and 3 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is elevated at approximately 480 mg per 100 g due to the soy and oyster sauce marinade, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is very low (estimated ~10), making it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in appropriate portions. The dish is a good source of zinc (4.2 mg/100 g), vitamin B12 (2.4 mcg/100 g), and selenium (26 mcg/100 g), nutrients important for immune function and neurological health. Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the saturated fat and cholesterol content from the lamb rib cut.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked lamb rib data (the closest approved source for this preparation), with sodium adjusted upward to account for the Chinese-style black pepper soy-based marinade. No exact match for black pepper lamb rack exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or other primary regional databases. GI is an analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed meat GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb rib (roasted, lean and fat, choice grade, USDA FDC ID 174352). Sodium is adjusted upward by approximately +150 mg/100 g to account for the soy sauce and oyster sauce marinade typical of Chinese-style black pepper lamb rack preparation. 2. Carbohydrate_g of 3.0 g/100 g reflects the small contribution from the black pepper sauce (soy sauce, oyster sauce, sugar); plain cooked lamb rib contains negligible carbohydrate. 3. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 for cooked lamb rib per USDA data; this is confirmed zero per the USDA source for this cut and preparation. 4. GI of 10 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for black pepper lamb rack exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Halal status is marked false as lamb rack from a Chinese seafood restaurant context cannot be assumed halal without explicit certification. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are sourced from USDA SR Legacy cooked lamb rib data and are not sentinel values. 7. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is set to 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked lamb rib (naturally zero for this cut and preparation method).
